mnemonics.title: TCP Header Fields Order
mnemonics.content: Please Sir, Read Carefully: Your Data Shouldn't Get Wasted Easily - Port fields, Sequence, Reserved, Control Flags, Window Size, Checksum, Urgent Pointer, Options/Data

mnemonics.title: TCP Flags Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: CAMS URP (like camera UR(P)) - Control, Ack, Push, Syn, Urgent, Reset, Fin. Or: Some People Enjoy Afternoon Tea - SYN, PSH, ACK, FIN, RST, URG (order may vary)

mnemonics.title: Sequence vs Acknowledgment
mnemonics.content: Seq tells WHERE I AM in my data stream. Ack tells WHAT I EXPECT from you next. Think: Seq = Send From, Ack = Expect Next.

acronyms.usage: Largest TCP payload (default 536 bytes). Negotiated in SYN using Kind 2 option.
Maximum amount of data TCP can send in one segment. Excludes TCP header.
Time a segment can exist in the internet before being discarded.
Used in connection establishment to sync sequence numbers.
Used in connection termination to indicate end of data stream.
Immediately terminates connection, often due to error.
Instructs receiver to deliver data to application immediately, bypass buffering.
Indicates urgent pointer field is valid, for out-of-band data.

visualTips.visualization: Think of TCP segment as an ENVELOPE: Header = Address/Stamp (contains Source/Dest Port, Seq#, etc.), Data = Letter inside. The stamp has fixed minimum size (20 bytes) but can expand up to 60 bytes (like adding extra stickers/options).

Data Offset is like the PAGENUMBER in a document - it tells you where the CONTENT starts. 5 means start at page 5 (20 bytes), 15 means start at page 15 (60 bytes = max). Each 'page' = 4 bytes (32 bits).
6 switches/buttons on a control panel: URG (red emergency), ACK (green confirm), PSH (yellow push), RST (red abort), SYN (blue sync), FIN (green done). Only one or combination can be ON.

Imagine stacking all 16-bit words in a column and adding them. Any overflow wraps around (carry back to LSB). Finally flip all bits (one's complement). This is like folding and inverting for error detection.

analogies.analogy: TCP segment is like a LETTER in a MAIL system, while an IP packet is like the ENVELOPE containing the letter, and the Ethernet frame is like the TRUCK that delivers it. The letter (segment) goes inside the envelope (packet) which goes on the truck (frame).
Sequence numbers are like PAGE NUMBERS in a book. If you receive pages 1-10, then 15-20, you know page 11-14 are missing. You ACK the next page you expect (page 11).

Window Size is like the NUMBER OF EMPTY SEATS at a restaurant. If you (sender) have 10 dishes (data) and the restaurant has 5 seats (window), you send only 5 dishes. When they eat and free seats, they send new window size.

Flags are like STATUS LABELS on a package: SYN = 'New shipment starting', ACK = 'Package received - send next', FIN = 'This is my last package', RST = 'Cancel everything', URG = 'Handle immediately', PSH = 'Don't wait - deliver now'.
